questions.scenario: A monitoring script needs to add two values: 128 (MB used) and 256 (MB reserved).
questions.question: Print the result of 128 + 256 using bash arithmetic expansion.
questions.description: $(( )) is bash's arithmetic expansion. It evaluates integer expressions. Variables inside don't need $ prefix (though it works). Division is integer-only — use bc for floats.
use_cases: Simple integer math without external commands
hints: No $ needed before variable names inside

questions.scenario: A loop counter needs to be incremented. Use the C-style increment syntax.
questions.question: Set COUNT=5, increment it using (( COUNT++ )), then print it.
questions.description: (( )) (without $) evaluates arithmetic but discards the result — used for side effects like increment/decrement. (( COUNT++ )) is equivalent to COUNT=$((COUNT+1)). Note: (( 0 )) returns exit code 1, useful in while loops.

questions.scenario: You need to check if 17 is divisible by 3 using the modulo operator.
questions.question: Print the remainder of 17 divided by 3 using arithmetic expansion.
questions.description: % is the modulo operator inside $(( )). Returns the remainder. Use it to check divisibility (result == 0), create rotating indices, or implement simple rate limiting in scripts.
use_cases: Check if a number is even/odd
Run an action every N iterations in a loop
Validate that file sizes are aligned to block boundaries

questions.scenario: A script checks if disk usage (85%) exceeds a threshold (80%). Use arithmetic comparison.
questions.question: Use (( )) to check if 85 is greater than 80 and print 'ALERT' if true.
questions.description: Inside (( )) and [[ ]] you can use -gt, -lt, -ge, -le, -eq, -ne for numeric comparisons. (( 85 > 80 )) returns exit code 0 (true) when the expression is non-zero. Combine with if or &&.
use_cases: Alert when disk usage exceeds threshold
Validate that backup file size is above minimum
hints: The (( )) returns 0 when expression is true

questions.scenario: You need to calculate 10 divided by 3 with decimal precision — bash integer math won't do it.
questions.question: Use bc to calculate 10 / 3 with 4 decimal places.
questions.description: bc is an arbitrary precision calculator. scale=4 sets decimal places. Feed expressions via here-string (<<<). Essential when bash's integer-only $(( )) is insufficient for monitoring thresholds or rate calculations.
use_cases: Calculate percentage disk usage with decimals
hints: Set scale=4 first for decimal output
